slotDescriptions: Determine the version of the product that can be released to the market
Take responsibility for removing blockers that affect the team's productivity
Determine how to organize and carry out work to complete the minimum viable product (MVP)
172.explanation: Product owner → market release version. Scrum master/PM → remove blockers. Development team → organize and carry out MVP work.

slotDescriptions: Estimate duration and include it in the project schedule
Assign story points to items in the backlog
Get team members' support to estimate duration or complexity
Make sure each project phase ends when an iteration ends and vice versa
352.explanation: Traditional → duration estimates. Agile → story points. All methodologies → team-supported estimating. Hybrid → align phases with iterations.

slotDescriptions: Assess the amount of time before the risk can impact one or more project objectives
Assess if the risk is related to other individual project risks
Assess how quickly the risk response needs to be implemented to be effective
Assess how long after a risk has occurred before its impact is discovered
356.explanation: Proximity → time to impact. Connectivity → related risks. Urgency → response speed. Dormancy → discovery delay.

393.text:
The following table shows the business value created and the development effort required to implement various features in a product.

| Feature | Business value | Effort (weeks) | Value / week |
| --- | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| Feature A | 120 | 2 | 60 |
| Feature B | 100 | 4 | 25 |
| Feature C | 80 | 2 | 40 |
| Feature D | 60 | 3 | 20 |

Which feature should the team prioritize first?
393.explanation: Prioritize by highest value-added (business value ÷ effort). Feature A delivers 60 value per week, the highest ratio.

slotDescriptions: Team members argue and are not openly collaborative
Members are reassigned to other projects
Team members are independent and work in silos
Members adjust their work habits with trust
Members are independent and work effectively
606.explanation: Storming → arguing. Adjourning → reassignment. Forming → silos. Norming → trust. Performing → effective teamwork.

slotDescriptions: Goal for this iteration
Development flow
Backlog items completed or not
Continue, stop, or start actions
608.explanation: Iteration planning → iteration goal. Daily meeting → development flow. Iteration review → backlog completion. Retrospective → start/stop/continue.

slotDescriptions: Availability of output formats of files to interface with data systems
Availability of resources for documentation and user manual preparation
Interfaces to medical devices require support from vendors
Needs regulatory compliance and clearance standards for incorporation
609.explanation: Technical → output formats. Management → documentation resources. External → vendor interfaces. Commercial → regulatory compliance.

slotDescriptions: Proactive in sharing ideas and training other team members
Forceful in pushing ideas even when others are uncomfortable
610.explanation: Extroverted → training/sharing behavior. Judging → decisive, forceful pushing of ideas.

slotDescriptions: Analyzed the threat, reduced probability of occurrence, and conducted additional tests
Determined the threat was severe and changed project strategies while reducing scope
Discussed the threat with the sponsor and bought insurance to handle it
Identified the threat and decided to review it periodically rather than take action
Studied the threat and found it is out of their authority and scope
611.explanation: Mitigate → reduce probability. Avoid → change strategy/scope. Transfer → insurance. Accept → periodic review. Escalate → beyond PM authority.

slotDescriptions: Site workers complain about working conditions but the PM cannot improve them
Stay updated on government instability and possible leadership change
Work in a rural area where people have different customs
Awarded a chair position to reform government policies after a new government forms
614.explanation: Decision making → worker complaints. Political awareness → government instability. Cultural awareness → rural customs. Negotiation → policy reform chair.

slotDescriptions: Routine information
Team discussion
Project deliverables
Negotiation
615.explanation: Chat rooms → routine updates. Video conferencing → team discussion. Bulletin boards → deliverables. Version control → negotiation artifacts.

617.text:
A project manager receives an update that the development flow is not going well. Some team members cannot start their work because the delivery is late. Looking at the Kanban board and the work in progress (WIP) limits, the project manager observes a bottleneck.

The board columns are: Code (4/5 WIP), Test (2/2 WIP — full), Document (1/3 WIP), Done.

Which queue title requires additional resources to relieve the bottleneck?
617.explanation: Test is at its WIP limit (2/2), blocking flow from Code. Add testing capacity to relieve the bottleneck.

slotDescriptions: Requirements are defined up-front before development begins
Changes are constrained as much as possible
Risk and cost are controlled by detailed planning
Key stakeholders are involved at specific milestones
Requirements are elaborated frequently during delivery
Changes are incorporated in real-time during delivery
Risk and cost are controlled as requirements and constraints emerge
Key stakeholders are continuously involved
619.explanation: Predictive → up-front requirements, constrained changes, detailed planning, milestone stakeholders. Agile → frequent elaboration, real-time changes, emerging constraints, continuous involvement.

slotDescriptions: Development team member — needs core agile ways of working
Scrum Master — needs facilitation and servant leadership skills
Product Owner — needs value optimization and backlog management skills
632.explanation: Fundamentals for developers, Scrum Master practices for SM, Product Owner practices for PO (three funded enrollments).

slotDescriptions: Workers are reluctant to finish tasks early so as not to set efficiency precedents
An activity is not ready to start when the predecessor finishes early, causing lost time
Procrastination in starting activities consumes slack built into durations
Workers use all time allocated for an activity regardless of actual need
673.explanation: Self Protection → avoid early finish. Dropped Baton → handoff gap. Student Syndrome → procrastination. Parkinson's Law → fill allocated time.

dragTerms: Appetite for change by leading by example
slotDescriptions: Work is decomposed into department silos, creating dependencies that prevent accelerated delivery
The Scrum master tries to use agile techniques and practices whenever possible
The organization's willingness to shift the way it views, reviews, and accesses employees
Decomposing work into iterative prototypes involves rework and needs change management
767.explanation: Silos → roadblock. SM modeling agile → appetite for change. HR/view shift → change tolerant. Iterative prototypes → change associated with agile.

slotDescriptions: Persuade a resistant stakeholder to attend a required workshop
Reach agreement on contract terms with a difficult vendor
Assess how a ministry reshuffle could affect project funding
Adapt communication style when working with an overseas joint-venture team
871.explanation: Influencing → persuade attendance. Negotiation → contract terms. Political awareness → ministry reshuffle. Cultural awareness → overseas JV team.

slotDescriptions: A required license for a design tool has expired
A key integration skill is missing on the team
Stakeholders keep adding scope during the sprint
Two developers disagree on a coding approach and it blocks progress
872.explanation: License → escalate for procurement. Skill gap → add specialist. Scope creep → reorder backlog with PO. Conflict → retrospective facilitation.

slotDescriptions: Define acceptance criteria and optimize product value
Manage dependencies and remove impediments between teams
Provide leadership to the team only when needed
873.explanation: PO → acceptance criteria/value. SM → dependencies/impediments. Servant leadership → leadership as needed.

slotDescriptions: Business requirements added by marketing
User requirements expressed as deliverable slices
Work selected for the current iteration
High-level strategic direction for the product
874.explanation: Business requirements → product backlog. User requirements → user stories. Iteration work → sprint backlog. Strategic direction → product vision.

slotDescriptions: are controlled as requirements and constraints emerge
are elaborated frequently during delivery
are incorporated in real-time during delivery
are continuously engaged throughout delivery
1139.explanation: Agile-style mapping: risks/costs controlled as constraints emerge; requirements elaborated frequently; changes in real-time; stakeholders continuously engaged.

slotDescriptions: The team needs to investigate a new approach
The team knows the acceptance criteria and needs to work backward
The team builds the demo first and adds features to the initial product
The business analyst has written use cases before the sprints started
1259.explanation: Spike → investigate. TDD → work backward from criteria. Continuous Delivery → demo first then features. BDD → use cases before sprints.

slotDescriptions: A team member explains they will not finish because they lack experience in the activity
The product owner receives new requirements and adds them to the backlog
The team commits more than 10% above capacity and presents it to the product owner
The team documents why velocity dropped about 40% in the last iteration
1312.explanation: Standup → assignment blocker. Planning → new requirements. Review → capacity commitment review. Retrospective → velocity root cause.

slotDescriptions: Unrealistic activity durations were used
Incomplete or incorrect activity dependencies
Incorrect sequencing of activities
Missing activities in the schedule
1315.explanation: Parametric → duration realism. PDM → dependencies. Critical path → sequencing. Decomposition/WBS → missing activities.

1329.text: A project manager is leading a project in which key performance indicators (KPIs) are measured through earned value management (EVM). Match each measurement on the left with the correct category on the right.
dragTerms: Project schedule performance index (SPI) is higher than 1.
Project cost performance index (CPI) is lower than 1.
slotDescriptions: Project variable is in good standing — First measurement
Project variable is in good standing — Second measurement
Project variable is performing poorly — First measurement
Project variable is performing poorly — Second measurement
1329.correctLabel: Good standing: CV positive (A), SPI > 1 (C) · Performing poorly: SV negative (B), CPI < 1 (D)
1329.explanation: CV > 0 → under budget (good). SPI > 1 → ahead of schedule (good). SV < 0 → behind schedule (poor). CPI < 1 → over budget (poor).

1335.text: An agile project manager is evaluating the method that will be used to display the value of the work performed to date. Which method will they select to display to the project stakeholder?

options.text: Burndown chart — remaining work over time
Burnup chart — completed value/work over time against total scope
Velocity chart — story points per iteration only
Gantt chart — task start and finish dates

1335.explanation: A burnup chart shows value/work completed to date against total scope — ideal for stakeholders tracking delivered value.

slotDescriptions: Bottom-up cost estimating for work packages
Review overall project performance with stakeholders
Monitor sprint workflow and WIP during iteration
Surface and remove impediments quickly
1359.explanation: WBS → bottom-up cost. Weekly meetings → performance review. Kanban → sprint flow. Daily standup → impediments.

1373.text: A team is experiencing difficulties and has decided to use a spike. On a burndown chart, how does a spike typically appear?

options.text: A flat horizontal segment — little or no completed work while the spike is time-boxed
A steep downward slope — faster than planned completion
A vertical drop to zero on the first day
A steady 45-degree line matching the plan

1373.explanation: During a spike the team investigates rather than delivering user stories, so completed work stays flat on the burndown.

slotDescriptions: Record observations and monitor — stakeholder still not participating
Exclude the stakeholder from further workshops
Ask another PM to manage the stakeholder relationship
Buy-in campaign and targeted engagement sessions
Raise the issue to senior management for a decision
1380.explanation: Accept → record/monitor. Avoid → exclude. Transfer → delegate relationship. Mitigate → buy-in/engagement. Escalate → senior management.

1387.text: A retail company must react quickly to deploy a mobile app; marketing has a long feature list but few specifications. The charter emphasizes deploying customer value fast. Which project life cycle approach should be chosen?
1387.explanation: Emerging requirements, speed to value, and frequent stakeholder feedback favor an agile life cycle.

slotDescriptions: External investors with little project visibility
Sales team concerned the project will disrupt current deals
Vendors providing committed resources and expertise
Senior management sponsoring and driving the initiative
1399.explanation: Investors → unaware. Sales → resistant. Vendors → supportive. Senior management → leading.

469.text:
The tables show the requirements for an agile project with the anticipated return on investment (ROI) and identified project risks, along with the analysis details.

Item Priority ROI ($)
Requirement 1 1 10,000
Requirement 2 1 8,000
Requirement 3 1 25,000

Risk Risk Impact ($) Risk Probability
Risk 1 5,000 75
Risk 2 4,000 50
Risk 3 20,000 45

What should be the priority order in the product backlog?
469.correctLabel: D. Requirement 3, Requirement 1, Risk 3, Requirement 2, Risk 1, Risk 2
